### Post by simonplexus on 2011-04-30
From looking at your drop logs it appears you want to accept connecitons on these ports (53, 3128?)

```

16141.894412]   1 udp packet from 192.168.16.71 port 44729 to 192.168.16.1 port 53
 [16141.250054]   1 tcp packet from 127.0.0.1 port 48917 to 127.0.0.1 port 3128
 [16142.699393]   1 tcp packet from 127.0.0.1 port 48919 to 127.0.0.1 port 3128
 [16142.464234]   1 udp packet from 192.168.16.71 port 49827 to 192.168.16.1 port 53

```If the iptables rules are on 192.168.16.1 and you wish to open port 443, 53 and 3128 you need to change your iptales command (you use --sport, should be --dport). Additionally its always a good idea to allow DNS on TCP too.
```

-A INPUT -p udp -m udp **--dport** 53 -j ACCEPT 
-A INPUT -p tcp -m tcp **--dport** 53 -j ACCEPT 
-A INPUT -p tcp -m tcp **--dport** 3128 -j ACCEPT 
-A INPUT -p tcp -m tcp **--dport** 443 -j ACCEPT 

```Also, remove the '--sport' option. This means that if i send traffic to 192.168.16.1 towards a port (say 22) then as long as I use a source port on my side as 3182 then your iptables will let me in.

### Post by simonplexus on 2011-04-30
Can you provide output of iptables-save after running commands I provided?

> but my understanding is when i use INPUT table i have to check incoming connections which is source portThis is not correct. on INPUT table you check destination port. Checking the source port only looks at the source port portion of the IP packet - the source port is usually random high

for example, simple firewall to allow all outbound connections, but only allow inbound connections for DNS, 443 and proxy port may look like this

```

#!/bin/bash
set -x

#clean any old rules
iptables -F
iptables -X
iptables -t nat -F
iptables -t nat -X
iptables -t mangle -F
iptables -t mangle -X

#allow all outbound TCP & UDP, block all inbound TCP & UDP
iptables -P INPUT DROP
iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT

#Allow UDP, DNS and Passive FTP
iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

#open up specific ports
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 3128 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 53 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p udp --dport 53 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT

```

If you want to default drop all outgoing, then apply --sport accept for specific ports in the OUTPUT chain.

### Post by Doug S on 2011-04-30
I think this packet:
>  
May 01 02:59:58 [26553.501470] eth2 1 tcp packet from 74.125.230.69 port 443 to 192.168.16.71 port 57056


Was actually accepted. It is in the log file because the log statement preceeds the rule that accepts the packet. I suggest to move the log rule to the last place for each chain.
 
the [n:m] part means n packets and m bytes went through that path of the tables.
You can reset or preset the counters. see this thread: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1715896](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1715896)

---

### Post by The Cog on 2011-04-30
simonplexus:
I don't think that's right. I assume that the yashar wants to use hid machine as a client. In this case, you don't need any of the "open up specific ports" rules. Outgoing requests will be allowed by the OUTPUT ACCEPT policy, and the corresponding replies will then be allowed in by the INPUT ESTABLISHED ACCEPT rule.

yashar:
You are not trying to run a DNS server are you?

Everyone:
Normally, a client will choose a high random port to send its request from, and the server listens on a well-known port number. So for example, when the client needs to look up a name with a DNS server, it sends a request to the DNS server. This outgoing packet will have a high random source port (e.g. 1234), and a destination port 53. The reply from the server will have a source port of 53 and a destination port 1234.Like this:
Query: client:1234 -> server:53
Reply: server:53 -> client:1234

Note that you should always allow packets from the loopback port back in, to allow local connections between applications (for instance, printing to the print spooler).
iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT

If you want to block outgoing connections, remember that you will generally want to allow outgoing DNS queries to the DNS server, and perhaps DHCP requests to the DHCP server in addition to other protocols you want to allow out.

### Post by Doug S on 2011-04-30
> Doug S, thanks, log level set to be 4 so it only logs drop packets. I do not understand. When one calls "LOG" from iptables there is no knowledge of the ultimate fate of the packet, that is decided by subsequent iptables rules.

### Post by The Cog on 2011-05-01
> [QUOTE]May 01 03:43:22 [29156.992868] 1 tcp packet from 127.0.0.1 port 35268 to 127.0.0.1 port 3128
May 01 03:43:22 [29156.992789] 1 tcp packet from 127.0.0.1 port 3128 to 127.0.0.1 port 35268
so just with loopback everything solved?! how?[/QUOTE]I assume you are running a squid proxy (that's what is normally on port 3128). So by allowing the loopback port to talk to itself, your browser is now able to connect to the squid proxy.

---
